bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes escalation risk and official inconsistency, using distancing language like "they say," labeling strikes "ruthless precision" and calling the episode a "confusing history," while right-leaning coverage frames decisive security action with valorizing verbs—"Warns," "Destroys," "eliminated"—and alarmist cues like "BEWARE!" and threats "never seen before" to justify force and portray Iran as a "rogue regime.
Pivotal dividing lines include whether mines were actually laid, independent verification, and differing tallies , while center coverage frames strikes as a measured "preemptive measure.
Both sides agree U.S. forces struck vessels and disrupted shipping and oil markets.
summary: On Tuesday, President Donald Trump warned he would escalate attacks if Iran mined the Strait of Hormuz, while U.S. forces destroyed 16 Iranian mine-laying vessels near the strait.
Facing superior conventional forces, Iran has relied on small craft carrying 2 to 3 mines each, while Operation Epic Fury launched Feb. 28 targets eliminating Iran's navy.
Strategic data show the Strait of Hormuz, about 21 miles wide, handles nearly 20% of global oil shipments, and Iran’s 6,000 sea mines could linger for months, blocking tanker traffic.
Shipping sources report that the Strait transit is effectively halted, insurers have refused coverage, and global energy markets reacted with oil over $100 and U.S. gas at $3.48 per gallon.

Policy responses include President Donald Trump's $20 billion reinsurance proposal and warnings that Iran will be hit 'twenty times harder,' while Iran's remaining small boats and mine-laying capacity still pose risks.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes human cost and institutional skepticism—foregrounding words like "wounded," "incensed," and the anonymous "as many as 150" figure to suggest undercounting and congressional alarm , while right-leaning pieces downplay harm and militarize the story with phrases such as "most injuries minor," "Operation Epic Fury," and skeptical tags like "they claim" to question anonymous sourcing; centrist outlets are more measured, repeating the Pentagon's "about 140" tally, "vast majority" minor, 108 returned to duty and eight severely injured.
Both sides agree troops were wounded and report competing tallies, reflecting deeper divides between oversight/human-cost framing and pro‑ military legitimation.
summary: Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell said on March 10 that about 140 U.S. service members were wounded over 10 days, with 108 returned to duty and eight severely injured.
A barrage of retaliatory rocket and drone strikes from Iran produced many U.S. injuries and killed seven soldiers in Kuwait and Saudi Arabia.

Military briefings show U.S. forces struck over 5,000 targets deeper into Iran's military base, while ballistic missile attacks fell 90% and one-way drones 83%, with B-1 bombers launched from a British base.
Brent crude spiked to nearly $120 on Monday as the International Energy Agency convened emergency talks, and the U.S. State Department authorized up to $40M for evacuation flights.

Iranian officials say the war will not end soon and they will not negotiate, while U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth warned Tuesday would bring the most intense strikes; polls show 53% of registered voters oppose military action.
title: About 140 US troops wounded in Iran war, 8 severely, Pentagon says

bias: Left outlets frame Hegseth’s "most intense day" rhetoric as alarming escalation—using terms like " war ," "live," "Secretary of War," "pummeling Tehran"—and foreground troop injuries and skepticism about claims of destroyed targets; right outlets celebrate forcefulness—using "vows," "we are winning," "on our timeline," "isolated"—and amplify 50+ ships and "thousands" of strikes as proof of success.
The center attributes claims with "says" and "very strong place," de-emphasizing partisan heat.
Key divides: casualty reporting, independent verification, and the meaning of Trump’s timeline; both sides agree the campaign is escalating and Iran vows to fight on.
summary: On March 10, 2026, U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth warned that Tuesday would be the most intense day of strikes on Iran, saying it's tied to the U.S.-Israeli military campaign.

Amid rising regional tensions, Iran has targeted energy infrastructure and traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, with a Revolutionary Guard spokesman threatening to stop Gulf oil exports and drones igniting a fire at Abu Dhabi's Ruwais refinery.
Pentagon officials described objectives to degrade missile, drone and naval threats as U.S. forces struck more than 5,000 targets, reducing ballistic missile attacks 90% and drone attacks 83%.
Seven U.S. service members have died, and Brent crude surged toward nearly $120, prompting G7 discussions on emergency reserves.
Hegseth said the campaign will continue on President Donald Trump 's timeline, warning Russia against involvement after a Trump-Putin call while the Kremlin's mediation offer remains on the table.
title: Hegseth says Tuesday ‘will be our most intense day of strikes’ on Iran

bias: Left frames the event as an institutional threat—using charged wording like "Opened Fire," "target," and calling it "intimidation" while linking it to synagogue shootings and the Middle East war; right amplifies geopolitical/terrorism alarm with vivid verbs such as "struck" and hyperbole like "Never Seen Anything Like This," foregrounding Iran/war links and reactions; center adopts restrained terms—"firearm discharged" and "national security incident"—and de-emphasized vehicle/photo specifics that left/right reported.

The pivotal divides are motive attribution and detail emphasis , yet all report shots fired, no injuries, an investigation and official condemnation, reflecting broader security-versus-contextual priorities.
summary: Around 5:30 a.m., officers responded to reports of gunfire at the U.S. consulate in Toronto and found evidence a firearm had been discharged.
Amid fears of attacks linked to the Middle East conflict, officials noted recent shootings at Toronto synagogues and a local boxing gym, raising concerns about Iranian proxy groups.
At 360 University Ave., television images and officers on scene showed apparent bullet damage while forensic officers and crime-scene technicians photographed evidence and checked security cameras.
The U.S. Department of State said it is investigating while Toronto police, who have no suspect information, urged members of the public to come forward with tips.

Officials warned the episode could become a major diplomatic incident amid international tensions, with Councillor Brad Bradford calling it 'This is completely outrageous' and others saying 'It's a potential game changer'.
title: Shots Fired at US Consulate in Toronto as Attacks on American Posts Spread

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the case as elite-driven abuse—using terms like "luxury," "opulent lifestyle," "shocked the real estate world," and "heinous abuse" and stressing prosecutors' broader victim count and documentary evidence to underscore systemic power.

By contrast, right-leaning pieces foreground celebrity ties and a sensational fall—invoking "brokers-to-the-stars," "twisted testimony," and the $240 million penthouse—leaning into law‑and‑order drama while de-emphasizing scale.
Center outlets emphasize legal process and appeals.
Both sides, however, converge on the guilty verdict and possible long sentences, revealing deeper divides over elite accountability versus scandal framing.
summary: On Sunday, March 9, in Manhattan, jurors convicted Oren Alexander, Tal Alexander, and Alon Alexander on all 10 counts after a five-week trial that began deliberations Thursday.

Prosecutors say the Alexander brothers carried out a sex-trafficking conspiracy from around 2008 to 2021 using luxury promises, leading to their December 2024 arrests at Brooklyn's Metropolitan Detention Center.
Prosecutors showed digital evidence including texts, emails, a blog post 'It's not rape if...', and videos, while eleven accusers testified about attacks dated between 2008 and 2021.
The brothers remain jailed at Brooklyn's Metropolitan Detention Center, face state charges in Florida and multiple civil plaintiffs, and their lawyers say they will appeal.

The case has prompted a ripple effect in the luxury real-estate industry, with about 24 pending civil lawsuits and top agents and developers severing ties, after the brothers' convictions of over $7 billion in sales since 2012.
title: Alexander brothers, top NY real estate brokers, convicted at sex trafficking trial

bias: Left coverage foregrounds a "rare opening" and repeatedly labels Clay Fuller "Trump-backed" to nationalize the race and highlight Democratic opportunity in a deep‑red district, while right outlets stress GOP agency—phrases like "GOP Voters Pick" and "overwhelming favorite"—celebrating the endorsement as legitimizing; center pieces de-emphasize partisan theater, focusing on how the contest "tests the power" of Trump and noting Fuller’s background amid a "crowded field" .
Disputes over Greene’s "resigned" status/"falling out" with Trump are a pivotal dividing line; all agree Fuller and Shawn Harris advance to an April 7 runoff.
summary: Clay Fuller and Shawn Harris will face each other in a runoff scheduled for April 15 to replace Marjorie Taylor Greene in Georgia's 14th District.
Greene resigned before January after falling out with President Trump, and a dozen GOP hopefuls prevented an outright winner, leading to a runoff.
Fuller drew over $1.8 million in outside ads, including support from two outside groups, while Harris raised over $4 million since mid-2025, according to AdImpact.
The April 7 runoff will decide who serves through January and briefly fill the vacancy, leaving the seat unfilled as Republicans navigate a historically narrow House majority.

The contest has become a test of President Trump's sway inside the GOP, with several candidates downplaying his endorsement even as Fuller benefited from it, while Harris called the race a 'once-in-a-generation opportunity' after his 2024 loss to Greene by nearly 30 points.
title: Trump-backed Fuller and Democrat Harris move to Georgia runoff to succeed Marjorie Taylor Greene

bias: Right-Leaning coverage adopts a skeptical, alarmist tone, using phrases like "Big Problem" and "Iran war " to frame the IEA proposal as a desperate intervention.
It highlights the 182 million barrel release from 2022 to underscore the scale of perceived policy failure.
While left-leaning perspectives are de-emphasized, center-leaning reporting focuses on market stabilization, employing terms like "choppy" and "rebound" to describe the impact on Asian stocks.
Both perspectives agree the proposal is the "largest ever," reflecting a shared recognition of the event's historic magnitude amid global energy volatility and broader geopolitical divides.
summary: On March 11, oil prices saw volatility after a WSJ report that the International Energy Agency proposed the largest release of oil reserves in its history, causing global prices to fall.

Amid the war on Iran, supply routes have been disrupted, with US and Israeli strikes intensifying flows disruption, and Saudi Arabia boosting supplies via the Red Sea, though levels remain below what the Strait of Hormuz losses require.
The IEA proposed drawdown would exceed the 182 million barrels of oil released in two 2022 releases, with both contracts plunging over 11% on Tuesday.

G7 officials have gathered online to discuss a potential release of emergency oil stockpiles, while Tony Sycamore, market analyst with IG in Sydney, said crude will trade between $75-ish and $105-ish amid headline volatility.

Energy consultancy Wood Mackenzie warned of a roughly 15 million barrels per day supply hit, which could raise crude prices to $150 per barrel, and Abu Dhabi state oil company ADNOC shut the Ruwais refinery after a drone strike.
title: Oil prices fall as WSJ says IEA proposes largest oil stock release ever

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes Rogan’s repudiation of Trump’s Iran strikes, framing supporters as "betrayed" and echoing Rogan’s "it just seems so insane" with moral outrage and words like "Nuts!"; right-leaning coverage largely de-emphasizes the Iran critique and instead dramatizes Rogan’s combative exchange over Epstein, using sensational phrases such as "knock-down, drag-out" and foregrounding "murdered" skepticism.
Center coverage bridges those frames by highlighting an internal conservative split—reporting supporters "feel betrayed"—but with less partisan heat.
The dividing lines are the Iran strikes and Epstein questions; both agree Rogan’s comments are consequential, reflecting deeper cultural and geopolitical divides.
summary: On Tuesday, Joe Rogan criticized President Donald Trump’s Iran strikes as insane on his podcast, joining other podcasters in public criticism.
Against that backdrop, Joe Rogan criticized Trump’s Iran strikes, highlighting his prior support and campaign promises to end wars, which contrast with recent actions.

According to Associated Press casualty count, at least 1,230 people in Iran and seven U.S. service members have been killed since the strikes began, with 91% of MAGA voters supporting the campaign, despite 55% of Americans seeing no imminent threat from Iran.
A rift has opened in conservative circles, with former Fox hosts Tucker Carlson and Megyn Kelly denouncing the strikes while Sen. Lindsey Graham defends them.
On Tuesday, Rogan questioned whether actions were driven by outside interests, particularly Israel, and cited Shellenberger’s quote: 'They’re not acting on the basis of achieving regime change.
title: Joe Rogan: Trump supporters ‘feel betrayed’ by ‘insane’ Iran war

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes scientific promise and policy implications, highlighting p‑tau217 as "most promise" and framing the roughly 25‑year lead time as a way to "open the way" to earlier prevention and monitoring; right-leaning pieces dramatize the same facts with sensational rhetoric—terms like "breakthrough" and "lurking dementia risk"—and foreground original-study details to bolster a revelatory narrative; center reporting tends to assert "can detect" or "predicts" decisively, with caveats de-emphasized.

Dividing lines include methodological caveats, applicability beyond women, and ethical/clinical readiness; all sides agree the biomarker links to future dementia risk and could enable earlier intervention, reflecting ideological splits.
summary: 1- A new study suggests a blood test can predict dementia risk in women up to 25 years before symptoms appear. 2- Researchers found higher levels of the protein p-tau217, linked to Alzheimer’s brain changes, strongly predicted future cognitive decline. 3- The study of 2,766 women in the Women’s Health Initiative Memory Study by the University of California San Diego shows early warning signs of dementia can be detected decades before symptoms.
title: Blood test detects dementia risk in women 25 years before symptoms

bias: Left outlets frame Italy ’s win as a dramatic upset—highlighting homers by Teel, Antonacci and Caglianone and even inconsistent scores with language like "upset" and "stuns" to stress shock; right outlets emphasize humiliation, using terms such as "embarrassing," "massive upset" and claims of it being the season’s "most shocking" result to foreground national shame.

Center coverage de-emphasizes theatrics and stresses mechanics—"tiebreaker" scenarios and the U.S. being "in peril"—while player details are de-emphasized on the right; all agree Italy won and U.S. advancement is jeopardized, revealing spectacle-versus-procedural framing tied to cultural priorities.
summary: On Tuesday, March 10, 2026, Italy national baseball team defeated the United States national baseball team 8-6 in Houston, ending with Yankees captain Aaron Judge striking out with two outs in the ninth.
Italy built an 8-0 cushion by the sixth inning, using three early home runs and veteran right-hander Michael Lorenzen's shutout innings.

Chicago White Sox catcher Kyle Teel opened the scoring with a second-inning solo homer but exited with an apparent leg injury, while the Team USA offense rallied late with homers from Pete Crow-Armstrong and Gunnar Henderson.
With Italy now 3-0, Team USA dropped to 3-1, making its advancement dependent on the Pool B finale between Italy and Mexico.

Ahead of Wednesday's Italy–Mexico matchup at 7 p.m. streaming on TUBI, a Mexico win could create a three-way 3-1 tie decided by runs allowed per defensive out: U.S. 11 runs/54 outs, Italy six runs/27 outs, Mexico five runs/24 outs.
title: Italy Upsets Team USA 8-6 in Major World Baseball Classic Shock

summary: On Monday, U.S. Attorney Eric Grant announced Ari J. Lauer, 61, former outside counsel for DC Solar, was sentenced to 11 years and five months after pleading guilty to 23 felony counts.
Between 2011 and 2018, DC Solar, Benicia-based solar company, solicited investors using fraudulent techniques and promoted federal tax credits and lease-back deals, prosecutors said.

Records show transactions involving 17,000 generators worth about $2.5 billion; prosecutors say about 9,000 non-existent units were part of the scheme raising $759.4 million from investors, with $152.7 million transferred to DC Solar for related transactions.

U.S. Attorney Eric Grant said the scheme would not have operated without Ari J. Lauer; the California State Bar placed Lauer on involuntary inactive status, and Jeff Carpoff is serving 30 years plus $790.6 million restitution.

Prosecutors say DC Solar touted trailer-mounted mobile solar generators for emergency power, but conspirators concealed missing third-party lease revenue by moving investor funds, duping Berkshire Hathaway Inc.
title: Former Attorney for California Solar Firm Sentenced in $1 Billion Fraud Scheme

summary: On Monday, BETA Technologies was selected to participate in the Federal Aviation Administration's eVTOL Integration Pilot Program and was approved for seven of the eight projects it applied for.
Created by the Trump administration last year, the pilot program aims to fast-track electric aircraft through the Federal Aviation Administration and expand aviation access, company leaders said.
BETA will deploy its ALIA aircraft for cargo and medical logistics in upstate New York and Vermont, partnering with Metro Aviation and the Port Authority of New York and New Jersey.

Market reaction was immediate as BETA's stock over 10% jumped after Monday, with CEO Kyle Clark calling the initiative 'a huge opportunity' and 'advancing our entire business model by more than a year'.
The FAA selected eight projects that together span 26 states, with operations planned in at least 10 states under the national pilot program.
title: Beta Technologies selected for federal pilot program, set to fly in Vermont this year

summary: Indicted Tuesday, the two former NYPD patrol officers Justin McMillan and Justin Colon were arraigned later in Brooklyn federal court on felony conspiracy and deprivation-of-rights charges.

After the state case lapsed on discovery and speedy-trial issues, state charges including burglary, forcible touching and official misconduct were dismissed, prompting the federal civil-rights indictment.
On July 20, 2024, prosecutors say officers Justin McMillan and Justin Colon shut off bodycams, took a building key, returned eight hours later, and McMillan stole about $200 and groped the woman.
Both men pleaded not guilty and were freed without bond; they resigned from the NYPD in March 2025 and face travel restrictions and passport surrender later this week.
Assistant U.S. attorney Erin Reid said the evidence is very strong, citing video footage, witnesses and a 911 call, while defense attorneys disputed the lapsed state case.
title: Ex-NYPD Officers Accused of Brothel Key and Robbing a Sex Worker Face Federal Charges

bias: Coverage splits along a visual‑versus‑operational line: left outlets foreground vivid, atmospheric visuals and aerial footage—using terms like "massive" and "smoke filled the skies"—while right outlets amplify threat‑centric, sensational language such as "giant inferno" and "burns for hours," sometimes framing the blaze as photo‑worthy.
Center coverage de‑emphasizes spectacle and instead foregrounds uncertainty about injuries, shipping impacts, and multi‑agency coordination.

This divergence—sensational atmosphere versus procedural uncertainty—reveals priorities of public drama versus impact and order, even as all note Coast Guard /local responders, visible smoke, and an investigation into the cause.
summary: On Tuesday, local firefighters worked to extinguish a scrap metal barge fire off Reedy Island, with no injuries reported, as footage showed flames and smoke.
The U.S. Coast Guard said a tugboat was moving the unmanned barge and that the cause of the fire is not yet known and remains under investigation.
Chopper 6 footage showed flames and smoke rising while fire boats and tugs sprayed water onto piles of scrap metal aboard the barge on Tuesday.
Officials reported no injuries as local fire departments and response crews worked for hours while ship traffic remained unaffected by the blaze.
Action News asked viewers to stay with 6abc.com as NBC10 said it was working to learn more and continued aerial reporting during the fire incident on Tuesday.
title: Massive fire breaks out aboard scrap metal barge on Delaware Bay

summary: Last week, Vermont State Auditor Doug Hoffer's audit found gaps in Vermont's child care oversight that pose risks to child safety and federal funding, warning the Office of Child Care could enforce penalties.

The audit found that Vermont's background-check system was incomplete and unacceptably lengthy, while an outdated state IT tracking system and gaps in retrieving out-of-state information undermined compliance.

Auditors documented that the state permitted staff to start work with temporary clearances before background checks cleared and officials often failed to confirm fixes, leaving parents of enrolled children unaware.
Federally funded programs were also cited when the audit found delays and citations that should have been classified as 'serious' pose risks to children and federal funding.
Officials expect some work to be finished by the end of this year as Janet McLaughlin said her team is issuing a new IT contract now and a digital system may arrive next year.
title: Audit finds gaps in Vermont child care oversight pose risks to children, threaten federal dollars

bias: Left-Leaning outlets emphasize establishment continuity, using "longtime" to bolster experience while framing the victory as defeating a "young" challenger.
Conversely, right-leaning coverage highlights a "generational struggle," focusing on the incumbent’s age of "78" to portray him as entrenched and using "tops" to diminish the win.
While the right mentions his "Jan. 6 Committee" role to signal partisan friction, the left de-emphasizes this, focusing instead on concurrent wins like Cliff Johnson’s.
Center-Leaning reports provide neutral framing, characterizing the "long-shot" bid as an attempt to "fend off" the incumbent.
Both sides agree on the landslide victory, reflecting divides over political longevity.
summary: On Tuesday, Representative Bennie Thompson secured the Democratic nomination for Mississippi 's 2nd Congressional District, defeating two challengers including Evan Turnage, a 34-year-old antitrust attorney and former Senate counsel.
At 78, Thompson entered the primary as a long-serving lawmaker with national profile, while challengers framed their bids as part of a generational shift and tech regulation debate.
Thompson's long tenure helped his appeal at the ballot box as his 17 terms made him an institution in Mississippi, with voters like Selket Myles from Jackson trusting his experience and local ties.
Because Mississippi's 2nd Congressional District is heavily Democratic, Thompson's win reinforces incumbent advantages in the short term, making him likely to prevail in November, analysts say.
Younger challengers have already notched notable victories, but even unsuccessful bids like Thompson's influence debates and signal shifting expectations among Democratic voters.
title: Longtime US Rep. Bennie Thompson defeats young Democratic challenger in Mississippi primary

summary: The FDA 's March 5 warning letter to Novo Nordisk, pharmaceutical company, requested details on steps taken to comply with PADE reporting after failures to report semaglutide side effects.
An inspection in last year at Novo Nordisk's U.S. headquarters in Plainsboro, New Jersey, was conducted as part of the Bioresearch Monitoring Program, FDA investigators found problems.

The FDA cited three deaths among patients receiving semaglutide, including one suicide, and noted a patient reporting suicidal ideation, but the agency did not assert the medications caused the deaths.

The FDA gave Novo Nordisk two weeks to outline corrective actions, and the company said on Tuesday it has been 'working diligently' since last year's inspection, with Anna Windle pledging to address requests expeditiously.
The FDA's monitoring program depends on complete ADE submissions, with January's boxed-warning removal request framing the March 5 letter amid ongoing oversight.
title: FDA sends warning letter to Novo Nordisk over failure to report deaths, adverse side effects of GLP-1s

bias: Left coverage frames the action as an administrative evacuation, stressing "evacuation" language and the State Department's authorization of "up to $40M" amid the "Iran war," while right coverage de-emphasizes funding and instead spotlights triumphant repatriation, touting "over 43,000" returned, "directly assisted" nearly 30,000 and that citizens were "safely returned.

A pivotal divide is whether emphasis falls on funding and policy mechanics—with center noting emergency stockpile use and that the department "responds to criticism"—or on scale and success; both agree the State Department aided large numbers during Middle East unrest, reflecting ideological priorities.
summary: On Monday, the U.S. State Department authorized up to $40 million from an emergency fund normally reserved for diplomatic and consular staff to pay for evacuation charter flights for Americans in the Middle East .
After strikes on Iran closed airspace and cancelled flights, the U.S. advised Americans in 14 countries to leave, with over 40,000 returning since Feb. 28.

The department has organized more than two dozen charter flights that carried thousands of U.S. citizens, but charters continue to operate with less than 40% occupancy as commercial flight options improve.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio waived the reimbursement requirement for private Americans last week, the administration said it will work with Congress if more funding is needed, and the department's 24/7 task force has assisted more than 23,000 Americans.

Lawmakers and travelers criticized the State Department's delayed response, with Robert Mills urging better preparedness: 'If something should ever happen again, be better prepared for American citizens who are abroad so that we can make it back home'.
title: State Department authorizes up to $40M for Middle East evacuation flights
Leading economics organization no longer affiliated with Larry Summers
Microsoft backs Anthropic in lawsuit against Trump admin

bias: Left-Leaning headlines emphasize the procedural act—the FBI raising the reward to $1,000,000—often tersely framing the move as urgent while de‑emphasizing victim and investigatory detail.
Right-Leaning pieces stress menace and punishment, using charged phrasing like "brutal," "hunt," "capture," and sensational cues and sometimes conflating other fugitives to heighten threat.
Center coverage sits between them, naming the victim and legal status .
Pivotal divides include victim identification, foreign‑hiding implications, and mixing cases; all sides nonetheless concur on the reward increase and call for public tips.
summary: On Tuesday, the FBI Los Angeles Field Office and Los Angeles Police Department announced a $1 million reward for information leading to Omar Alexander Cardenas's arrest.
Previously, authorities noted the FBI initially offered $100,000 when Cardenas joined the FBI's Ten Most Wanted list in 2022 and later raised it to $250,000 after a federal unlawful-flight warrant.

Investigators detailed that Cardenas allegedly fired several rounds from a semi-automatic handgun on Aug. 15, 2019, killing Jabali Dumas outside Hair Icon Barber Shop, while video footage and a recovered vehicle linked to the killing were obtained.
LAPD Chief Jim McDonnell urged people to call the FBI tip line 800-225-5324 or use tips.fbi.gov, saying 'A warrant for Cardenas' arrest remains active with full extradition'.

Authorities describe Cardenas as 5-foot-6 to 5-foot-7, 240–300 pounds with dark brown hair, brown eyes, nickname 'Dollar', suspected Pierce Street Gang member, and possibly linked to Pacoima Van Nuys Boys/Anybody Killas.
title: $1M Reward For Capture Of LA Murder Suspect, FBI's 'Most Wanted' Fugitive

bias: Left-Leaning pieces frame the event as an "attack" that "deepens Mideast violence," using emotive, escalation-focused language and human-impact emphasis, while right-leaning coverage uses blunt verbs like "hits/struck," centers the U.S. facility, signals threat to American interests and notes syndicated distribution; center and right explicitly attribute the claim to an external report and unnamed U.S. sources , whereas the left repeats the substantive claim without that attribution; all sides report a drone struck the facility as suspected retaliation by pro‑Tehran militias tied to U.S.-Israeli actions, but casualty, official, and local-reaction details are de-emphasized.
summary: On Tuesday, a drone struck the U.S. diplomatic support center near Baghdad airport, and a U.S. State Department internal alert ordered individuals to 'duck and cover', the Washington Post reported.
Suspected retaliation by pro-Tehran militias followed U.S. and Israel attacks on Iran that began on February 28, and the strike was likely carried out by the Islamic Resistance in Iraq.
Six drones were launched toward the compound; five drones were shot down while one drone hit near a guard tower, prompting a State Department alert to 'duck and cover'.
Iran's U.N. ambassador said U.S.-Israeli strikes killed more than 1,300 civilians, Israel reported 11 civilian deaths, and the U.S. military confirmed seven service members were killed.
Iran has responded with strikes on Israel and U.S. bases, while President Donald Trump said the strikes aimed to eliminate 'imminent threats' and Reuters reported as many as 150 U.S. troops wounded.
title: Drone hits U.S. diplomatic facility in Iraq as Mideast violence deepens

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes accountability and conflict—calling out that Hyde‑Smith "blocked" Colom and framing the race as a "showdown" centered on the prosecutor—while right-leaning pieces valorize incumbency and combat, using words like "cruised," "clash," and "vanquished" to portray an easy, triumphalist victory and a personal battle.
Centrist accounts tend to neutralize the language, repeating "blocked" as context and noting the timeline to Biden’s prior nomination.
The pivotal dividing line is Hyde‑Smith’s prior opposition to Colom’s judgeship, which triggers starkly different tones; both sides nonetheless concur on winners and the November rematch.
summary: On Tuesday, CNN projected Sen. Cindy Hyde-Smith and Scott Colom won their primaries, setting a November rematch after Hyde-Smith blocked Colom's federal judgeship nomination.
Using her blue-slip tradition, Hyde-Smith blocked the nominee, citing his support from George Soros and his role supporting transgender rights, Calvert said.
In the Democratic primary, Scott Colom defeated Albert Littell and Priscilla W. Till while highlighting his prosecutor record and 2015 district attorney race win over a nearly 30-year incumbent.
The matchup raises the possibility that Hyde-Smith will help the GOP retain Senate control, while Colom could become the first Black senator from Mississippi since Reconstruction.
Despite the GOP advantage, Democrats say they are buoyed by modest gains last year and state legislative pickups, reflecting shifting political dynamics in Mississippi.
title: U.S. Senate Incumbent Cindy Hyde-Smith wins Mississippi’s Republican primary election

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes governments weighing "emergency measures" and frames the story around a "war rages" narrative that spotlights supply insecurity and urgent policy options, whereas right-leaning pieces deploy alarmist tactics—using all-caps "EMERGENCY" and terms like "SEVERE DISRUPTIONS"—to stress crisis, justify calls for more domestic production and push back on restrictive energy/climate policies; center reporting foregrounds meeting specifics and scale, using phrases like "roils markets" and "largest ever" to portray coordinated technical responses.
Both sides agree the IEA convened over Gulf-linked disruptions and possible reserve releases, underscoring geopolitical stakes versus policy priorities.
summary: On Tuesday, the International Energy Agency convened an extraordinary meeting in Paris to assess supply and whether to release emergency stocks, hosting G7 energy ministers at its Paris headquarters.
Amid the Middle East war, tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has slowed and production curtailed, causing crude prices to jump 30 percent on Monday to nearly $120 before retreating.
IEA member countries currently hold over 1.2 billion barrels of public emergency stocks plus roughly 600 million barrels of industry inventories, meeting the 90 days emergency stock obligation.
G7 finance ministers discussed a potential release on Monday, while Lescure said the IEA was asked to update stock data and prepare scenarios to stay ready, but the situation was 'not there yet'.
With about 100 million barrels of oil are consumed globally every day, officials say coordinated releases could shape markets as Iran vows to halt Gulf exports, risking supply disruptions.
title: IEA Mulls Emergency Action To Unleash Oil Reserves

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes procedural caution and institutional framing—highlighting the 'national security incident' investigation and calling the event a 'reprehensible act' while de-emphasizing granular perpetrator details—whereas right-leaning pieces foreground law-and-order escalation, using vivid wording like 'shots fired' and pushing 'beefed-up protection' by stressing two assailants, a white SUV, a handgun and an attack at about 4:30 a.m.

Center coverage sits between them, noting 'no injuries' and probing links to recent synagogue shootings, with officials saying it's 'fairly obvious' sites need vigilance; all agree on the shooting, increased security and a national-security probe.
summary: Police found spent shell casings and damage to the U.S. consulate in Toronto after a shooting early Thursday morning.
Canada will increase security at U.S. and Israeli consulates and embassies in Toronto and Ottawa in response to the incident.
Police say two men fired shots at the consulate from a white SUV and fled, but the motive is unclear and they are investigating any links to recent synagogue shootings.
title: Canada stepping up security at US, Israeli diplomatic buildings after shootings

summary: Ivanna Lisette Ortiz, 35, was charged Tuesday with attempted murder and multiple felony firearm offenses after gunfire struck Rihanna 's gated Beverly Hills home Sunday afternoon.

Investigators say Ortiz drove to the area from Florida and public records list an Orlando address; she is a licensed speech‑language pathologist, but authorities say a motive and any link to Rihanna remain under investigation.
Officers located and stopped a vehicle in Sherman Oaks, leading to an arrest after police said an AR‑15‑style rifle was used and police dispatch audio recorded roughly 10 shots.

A court hearing Tuesday resulted in swift filings and restrictions: Ortiz faces 14 felony counts, Jamarcus Bradford withdrew a not-guilty plea and postponed arraignment to March 25, and a judge barred Ortiz from firearms and ordered her to stay away from Robin Fenty and Rakim Mayers.

Prosecutors tacked special sentencing allegations onto each count, District Attorney Nathan Hochman said the charges involve 10 victims including Rihanna's family and neighbors, recalling a 2018 intruder case.
title: Woman charged with attempted murder in shooting at Rihanna's Los Angeles home

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the defense agreement through the personal alliance of "closest allies," portraying the legislative win as a political "victory" for the Trump administration.
Conversely, right-leaning coverage emphasizes a strategic "defense agreement" that "allows" for an expanded footprint, focusing on the regulatory framework for U.S. personnel.
While center-leaning reports introduce skepticism by labeling the deal "controversial" and the U.S. a "North American giant," they de-emphasize specific reasons for opposition.
Both sides converge on the "victory" narrative and the large legislative majority, reflecting a shared focus on shifting geopolitical influence and strengthening ties in Latin America .
summary: Paraguay's Chamber of Deputies approved a defense agreement allowing temporary US military presence for training and assistance.
The agreement establishes legal framework for US security forces in Paraguay and grants them criminal jurisdiction.
Some opposed the deal, citing concerns over sovereignty and immunity for foreign troops, while officials argued it strengthens security cooperation.
title: Paraguay Lawmakers Approve a Defense Agreement that Allows an Increased US Military Presence

bias: Left-Leaning coverage foregrounds worker harm and unfolding danger—using phrases like "employees," "hospitalized," "hazmat situation unfolds" and stressing symptoms such as dizziness and shortness of breath—while right-leaning pieces amplify alarm with shorthand "hazmat," "hazardous materials incident," "scary situation" and outcome-focused verbs like "sends" or counts like "multiple people hospitalized.
The center emphasizes attribution and mitigation—reporting a "building materials" reaction, an "unknown odor," named responders and "minor injuries.
Pivotal divides are cause attribution and symptom detail; both sides agree responders acted and staff were taken to hospitals, reflecting worker-safety versus public-order priorities.
summary: On March 10, 2026, five Disneyland employees were hospitalized after an unknown odor in a backstage area near Star Tours, with crews arriving around 12:30 p.m.
Disneyland officials said building materials used by a contractor produced an unexpected reaction that caused the odor backstage.
Employees reported minor pain, shortness of breath and dizziness, and several cast members were treated by on-site paramedics with some released at the park.
Adjacent onstage areas were temporarily closed and cleared of guests as a precaution, and Disneyland said those areas were expected to reopen soon.
Anaheim Fire Department confirmed a hazardous-materials situation in a backstage area, and officials said no further details were immediately available as investigators continued assessing the scene.
title: Disneyland hazmat incident sends 7 employees to the hospital

summary: On March 9, 2026, U.S. District Judge John R. Adams granted East Palestine residents Robert Figley and Barbara Adams permission to join the federal consent-decree lawsuit against Norfolk Southern, allowing them to raise concerns about environmental testing, contamination, and the proposed settlement.

Residents sought intervention after independent testers found dangerous contamination persists in East Palestine, challenging company and Environmental Protection Agency claims, while whistleblowers alleged flawed sampling ignored wind patterns.

Independent testing and FOIA material show Scott Smith repeatedly found high dioxin levels and records from the Government Accountability Project supported evidence of neglected oversight and worsening contamination.

The intervention could allow EPA whistleblowers their day in court, exposing oversight failures and enabling challenges to testing, settlement adequacy, and data access amid Norfolk Southern and government defendants' opposition.

The timing places the ruling nearly three years after the 2023 derailment, as media coverage and advocacy continue highlighting contamination concerns and testing results, with Lesley Pacey noting emerging cancer clusters.
title: Judge allows East Palestine residents to intervene in train derailment lawsuit
Lebanon's latest conflict brings rare public backlash against Hezbollah as war flares again
Mamdani Hosts Palestinian Activist Mahmoud Khalil for Ramadan Dinner at Gracie Mansion

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes the humanitarian emergency—using phrases like "relentlessly pounding" and "upended on a massive scale" and highlighting 100,000 displaced in 24 hours—while right-leaning pieces frame Israeli action as "launched air attacks," "in an effort to target" Hezbollah , spotlight the larger "nearly 700,000" total and stress the registration-based methodology.
The left uses emotive, accusatory language; the right adopts operational, cautionary wording; center coverage is more descriptive and de-emphasized moral framing.
Key dividing lines are responsibility attribution and figure methodology, though all cite UNHCR, shelters and widespread displacement.
summary: On Tuesday the United Nations said nearly 700,000 people fled their homes in Lebanon in just over a week, with more than 100,000 leaving within 24 hours.

The cross-border escalation began after Iran-backed Hezbollah attacked Israel following the killing of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, and Israeli strikes since March 2 have killed at least 486 people, including 84 children.
WFP Lebanon country director Anne Valand said the World Food Programme activated food and cash aid but urgently needs more funding as UNHCR operations in Lebanon are only 14 percent funded.

Around 120,000 displaced were sheltering in government-designated collective sites while others stayed with relatives or searched for accommodation; 65,000 Syrians returned from Lebanon since early March, with many heading to neighbouring Syria.

UNHCR's Karolina Lindholm Billing said 'Lives have been upended on a massive scale', and the World Health Organization warned overcrowding in shelters raises disease outbreaks and disrupts routine health services.
title: Israeli Strikes Pound South of Beirut, Displacing More than 100,000 in a Day

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the ruling as enforcement against companies "skirting" state gambling laws, highlighting other platforms like Polymarket and quoting the judge’s "avoid absurdity" phrase to suggest regulatory evasion; center-leaning pieces instead emphasize legal mechanics—denial of a preliminary injunction, that Kalshi "must adhere" and that the Commodity Exchange Act does not "preempt" state law—using neutral verbs like "orders.

Right-Leaning treatment is largely de-emphasized in the provided analyses, limiting direct contrast, while left and center nevertheless agree on the core facts, reflecting a split between regulatory-accountability and legal-procedure priorities.
summary: U.S. District Court Judge Sarah Morrison in Ohio found on Monday that Kalshi's sports prediction markets amount to gambling and should fall under state regulation.

Kalshi argued the federal Commodity Exchange Act preempts enforcement of Ohio law by treating its sports contracts as swaps, but states including Nevada and Massachusetts have resisted, citing state governments' control over sports betting.
Kalshi sought an injunction to block enforcement by the Ohio Casino Control Commission, which argues the company operates as an unlicensed sportsbook facilitating bookmaking.
Kalshi said it will appeal the decision, while Ohio Attorney General Dave Yost celebrated the ruling Tuesday and rejected Kalshi's preemption argument.
The dispute could last several years, keeping Kalshi and Polymarket operating as Kalshi recently won a Tennessee ruling blocking Nashville, creating a split among federal courts.
title: Ohio judge orders Kalshi to follow state betting laws

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the event with skepticism—using "claims" and noting lack of Iranian corroboration while emphasizing possible escalation with the U.S.—whereas right-leaning pieces foreground operational success, using "eliminated," highlighting additional strikes and language like "precise intelligence" and "continue to pursue.
Center-Focused accounts emphasize IDF confirmation and "real-time emerging intel" as authoritative.

Pivotal divides are independent verification, the reported scope of operations, and the framing of intelligence; all sides nonetheless report Israel 's announcement that newly appointed Abu al-Qassem Baba’iyan was killed, reflecting deeper ideological and geopolitical rifts.
summary: On Sunday, Israeli forces announced they eliminated Abu al-Qassem Babayian, the newly appointed military secretary to Iran's Supreme Leader, during a security assessment at Israel's defense headquarters in Tel Aviv.
The IDF says its campaign aims to continue targeting Iran's leadership, including successors, after killing Ayatollah Ali Khamenei on Feb. 28, during Operation Roaring Lion/Epic Fury.

Inside the meeting, senior security officials including Eyal Zamir, Amir Baram, Shlomi Binder and representatives from Mossad and Shin Bet heard Israel Katz praise the operation and urge, 'Keep hunting them all.'

Iran's Assembly of Experts will soon gather in Qom to select a new supreme leader, with a member saying the body would meet 'within the next 24 hours'; Mojtaba Khamenei, 56, is seen as a leading successor who Israeli reports say may be injured but alive.
Gen. Effie Defrin told reporters that the Israeli Air Force targeted the Assembly of Experts premises on March 3 and warned participants in the successor selection meeting they could be targeted.
title: Israel Says Iran’s Top Military Secretary Killed

bias: Left coverage frames the suit as an existential alarm—using language like "threats to free and fair elections," " military ‑election plot," "armed officers" and "troops at the polls" to emphasize intimidation, while right coverage foregrounds procedural irregularity—phrases such as "alleged unanswered FOIA requests" and "agents at polls" cast the case as records scrutiny and political strategy; center reporting instead stresses the legal/FOIA angle, using "failed to adequately respond" and noting the DNC’s roughly 11 requests.

Crucially, none include on‑the‑record agency comment or deep legal analysis on whether such deployments would be lawful, a gap both sides share even as their interpretations echo broader divides over state power and election legitimacy.
summary: The DNC sued the Trump administration on Tuesday in the U.S. District Court for the District of Columbia after 11 FOIA requests in October to DOJ, DHS, and DOD prompted no response, seeking records on federal polling site plans.

The DNC argued the requests were necessary because of repeated threats to election integrity, citing President Donald Trump's calls to 'nationalize' voting and officials refusing to rule out armed agents at polls .

The lawsuit compiles statements and actions the party says justify FOIA enforcement, citing Trump's remark about seizing machines and the FBI's Fulton County ballot seizure, while DOJ, DHS and DOD did not respond.

Democratic state attorneys general are training for worst-case election interference scenarios, including ballot seizures and federal agents at polling places, amid ongoing preparations for the 2026 elections.
Despite the legal ban, Democrats' concerns persist because of President Donald Trump's calls to 'nationalize' voting and the FBI raid in Fulton County, Georgia, ahead of the 2026 midterm elections.
title: DNC sues Trump administration for any plans to deploy federal agents at polling sites
